notes.anchor: This is a contest about kingship
notes.note: Cicero frames the civil war not as a constitutional struggle but as a contest for sole power (regnum) between two would-be monarchs, casting Pompey as the "more modest king" and Caesar as the worse. This stripping-away of republican pretence is one of his bleakest private verdicts on the war.

notes.anchor: he will win in Sulla's manner and after Sulla's example
notes.note: L. Cornelius Sulla, victor in the civil war of the 80s BC, became byword for victory followed by proscriptions, confiscations, and dictatorship; Cicero dreads that a Caesarian or Pompeian triumph will repeat that bloodshed.

notes.anchor: rather on Malta or in some other little town like it
notes.note: Malta (Melita), a quiet island away from the fighting, recurs in these letters as Cicero's preferred neutral refuge; he hopes to sit out the war there rather than join Pompey's battle-line.

notes.anchor: the business of Dolabella on the Adriatic side and of Curio on the side of the Strait
notes.note: Cicero's son-in-law Dolabella commanded for Caesar in the Adriatic, and Curio was heading for the Strait of Messina en route to Sicily; their goodwill might let Cicero slip away by sea. The "upper sea" (Adriatic) and the Strait mark the two possible escape routes out of Italy.
The Oppii of Velia were Atticus's "messmates" (contubernales) in that south-Italian town, distinct from the banking Oppii at Rome; Cicero leaves their handling to Atticus and his freedman Philotimus.
